query: good contacts in primary teeth predict permanent teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Preparation -- Selection of Crown
retrieved_docs.content: The operator can select the crown using different methods: Measuring the mesiodistal dimension of the tooth before tooth preparation Trial and error method Crown selection after tooth preparation

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pulp and periodontal outcomes are influenced by factors related to the injury and subsequent treatment. Favorable outcomes of TDIs in the primary teeth are the absence of symptoms and normal healing of the pulp and periodontal tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Favorable outcomes of pulp healing include a normal color of the crown or transient red/grey or yellow discoloration and pulp canal obliteration. There will be no signs of pulp necrosis and infection, and in immature teeth, root development will continue. Additional favorable outcomes include undisturbed development and eruption of the permanent successor teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Various studies have looked at the prognosis of traumatized primary teeth. A study by Soprowski found that 57% of luxated primary teeth showed no post-trauma sequelae, 25% became necrotic, 10% demonstrated calcific degeneration, and 8% became ankylosed. [23] The clinician should record all favorable and unfavorable outcomes at the initial appointment and subsequent follow-up appointments.

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The use of sealants should be guided by individual caries risk, not just eruption status. Although no diagnostic tool perfectly predicts caries development, a history of caries remains the most reliable indicator of risk for caries development. Resin-based sealants are generally more effective than glass ionomer cements due to superior sealing ability. Nevertheless, glass ionomer materials still have a role, especially in situations where rubber dam isolation is not possible. Even when dentin is involved, sealing can be effective if a tight bond is achieved. In primary teeth, sealants may perform comparably well as more invasive procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Unfavorable outcomes following TDI to primary teeth include the development of symptoms, signs of pulp necrosis such as sinus tract, gingival swelling, abscess, or increased mobility. Persistent dark grey discoloration with an additional sign of infection is an unfavorable outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: Cracked Tooth Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Early detection plays a crucial role in enhancing the prognosis of a cracked tooth. What initially appears as a minor, innocuous crack with mild symptoms can quickly develop into a complicated and extensive crack involving the pulp or root, rendering the tooth unrestorable. The extent, position, and direction of the crack determine the prognosis. Cracked teeth with normal pulp can be effectively managed with therapeutic measures such as direct composite splinting, placement of orthodontic bands, or indirect definitive cuspal coverage.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Dental caries is the most frequent oral disease of children worldwide, with a prevalence of 1% to 12% in developed countries and up to 70% in underdeveloped nations. [25] The management of caries in primary dentition is challenging and complex due to the need for behavior management measures and perdurable treatments that last until tooth exfoliation - both issues being solved by utilizing stainless steel crowns (see Image. Primary Dentition). Despite this, studies show that pediatric dentists are more likely to restore carious deciduous dentition using stainless steel crowns than general dentists (see Image. Stainless Steel Crown of 46). [30] A higher level of training in this treatment at the undergraduate level and access to postgraduate courses are required to increase general dentists' use of these effective appliances. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Mandibular Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: Second Molars – The mandibular second molars(tooth T, K) morphologically resemble the first mandibular molar of the permanent teeth. Its buccal surface is divided into the mesiobuccal, distobuccal, and distal cusps. The lingual cusps include the distolingual and the mesiolingual cusps. Numerous differences exist between the primary and permanent teeth, for example: Primary teeth are smaller Crowding is uncommon in primary teeth due to adequate space Primary teeth are whiter Pulp chambers of primary teeth are larger The enamel and dentin of primary teeth are thinner The area of contact of the primary molars are flat and broad The roots of the primary molar teeth are narrow and slender Pulp size relative to the crown is larger in primary teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Enamel-Dentine Fractures (uncomplicated crown fracture)
retrieved_docs.content: An uncomplicated crown fracture affects the enamel and dentine without exposing the pulp. These fractures generally affect upper central incisors' mesial angles or incisal edges. [14] The missing tooth fragment location should be investigated during history taking. A radiograph of the soft tissues is recommended if the fractured tooth is suspected to be embedded in the lips, cheeks, or tongue.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Crown-Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: A crown-root fracture affects the enamel, dentine, and root; the pulp may or may not be exposed (complicated or uncomplicated). Additional findings may include loose but still attached fragments of the tooth. A periapical or an occlusal X-ray should be taken.

retrieved_docs.title: Stainless Steel Crowns in Primary Dentition -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Primary teeth are relatively at a larger risk of caries than permanent teeth. They also have larger pulp with prominent pulp horns, which places exact demands on cavity design. Hence, a full-coverage restoration is required. Although stainless steel crowns have an aesthetic disadvantage, they are still the treatment of choice for the following reasons: Durability Efficiency Longevity Cost-effectiveness Reliability Even though other tooth-colored restorations are available in the market, they are costly or require more tooth reduction, increasing the chances of pulp exposure. [15] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographs are an important diagnostic tool for diagnosing TDIs and serve as a baseline when compared to follow-up appointments. Intra-oral radiographs may include periapical radiographs (size 0 sensor/film, paralleling technique) or occlusal radiographs (size 2 sensor/film). Conventional radiology may not be possible in young children due to compliance, and a clinician may consider alternative views, such as lateral obliques. However, it is important to consider if taking an image and exposing the child to radiation will positively affect the diagnosis or treatment provided. Clinicians must work within the ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) principles to minimize radiation doses. The use of pulp testing in children is challenging; it is subjective and relies on the child’s understanding and cooperation. [19] False-positive/negative results can be misleading in clinical situations and therefore are not recommended in the IADT guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- History and Physical -- Intrusive Luxation
retrieved_docs.content: When the tooth's apex is displaced toward or through the labial bone plate (away from the permanent tooth germ), the apical tip can be seen, and the image of the tooth will appear shorter (foreshortened) than the contralateral tooth. When the tooth's apex is displaced toward the permanent tooth germ, the apical tip cannot be visualized, and the image of the tooth will appear elongated.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The portion of the tooth that is visible in the oral cavity is termed the crown. The crown is connected to the tooth root, situated within the periodontium, and is not clinically visible to evaluate health without imaging technology (Figure 1). [2] The permanent dentition usually erupts in a predictable sequence, and the number of cusps, roots, and root canals varies by the individual tooth (Figure 2). Dental enamel forms the outer surface of the crown and is the hardest and most highly mineralized tissue within the human body. Carbonated hydroxyapatite crystals form the mineral component of dental enamel and contribute to approximately 96% of its weight. In addition, water contributes 3% of the weight of dental enamel, and the remaining 1% is composed of non-collagenous protein. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the close relationship between the apex of the primary tooth root and the underlying permanent tooth germ, there is potential for sequelae to the permanent tooth following orofacial and primary tooth trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to loss of teeth caused by an accident, indicating that dental injury resulting from an accident can lead to unintended physical contact.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.content: 0.0088 MG/MG Toothpaste. Across these preparations, sodium monofluorophosphate provides fluoride ions that inhibit demineralization and promote remineralization of tooth enamel, thereby reducing the incidence of dental caries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is classified as a secondary malignancy, indicating that the spread of cancer to these regional lymph nodes represents a secondary tumor rather than a primary disease. Recognizing this mapping is important for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 797
retrieved_docs.content: particular bones) are nested under a universal category of late effects, which also encompasses non‑fracture injuries such as burns and frostbite. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in systematically assessing and managing the long‑term sequelae of diverse traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2083
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental physical contact (event) is described as being due to verlies; tanden door ongeval, indicating that unintended contact can result in tooth loss caused by an accident. The phrase “Due to” is also associated with verlies; tanden door ongeval, reinforcing the causal link between accidental physical contact and dental loss in traumatic situations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2502
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to lymph nodes of the neck is classified as a secondary malignancy, reflecting the spread of a primary cancer to regional lymphatic tissue. Recognizing this metastatic involvement as a secondary malignancy is important for accurate staging, prognosis, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies.
